title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot and Inspector Spence discuss the conviction of James Bentley for the murder of Mrs. McKinty. Bentley was convicted based on evidence including a bloodstained cuff and stolen money found hidden outside the house. During his trial, Bentley altered his testimony regarding the blood, claiming he had merely touched the victim out of panic after finding her dead, which caused him to flee in terror. Spence notes that a waitress had described Bentley as a man who preferred isolated seating, suggesting his suspicious behavior might be a psychological response to panic rather than guilt, though he also considers the possibility that his alibi was fabricated by a lawyer.

Poirot argues that because Mrs. McKinty lived an unremarkable life without known enemies, the motive for the murder must involve an extraordinary killer. He posits that either the evidence against Bentley was fabricated to frame him, or Bentley was simply in the wrong place at the wrong time. Poirot suggests the investigation should shift focus from the victim to the character of the killer, questioning whether the true intention was to kill Mrs. McKinty or to use her death as a means to eliminate Bentley. Spence provides background on Bentley, noting he was the son of a deceased doctor, attended a small public school, worked in government during the war, and had lived with a domineering mother.
